Computational ecology · methods

The Geometry of Coexistence

Where will climate push Europe's wildlife into people? We turn human–wildlife conflict into a problem in the geometry of moving probability — and predict it without ever training on conflict data.

Optimal transport · spectral graph theory · persistent homology · maximum entropy  •  CMU Pre-College Final Project · Topic 25
7
conflict-prone taxa modelled across Europe
484 km
mean climate-forced range shift by 2081–2100
0.64
Spearman ρ, predicted vs observed range shift
0.0005
permutation p, friction predicts real interface
The idea

Conflict is moving mass meeting fixed pressure

As the climate warms, the comfortable habitat of a species has to move — generally poleward and uphill. But it moves across a continent whose land people have already claimed. Human–wildlife conflict — livestock loss, vehicle collisions, crop raiding, bears at the edge of town — happens exactly where that moving habitat is forced into human-dominated space and can't flow around it.

Almost every existing model predicts conflict by looking backward at past conflict records. We predict it forward, from mechanism: the transport, connectivity, and topology of climate-forced redistribution. Because the model never sees conflict data, testing it against reality is a true out-of-sample test — not a re-fit.

p(x) ∝ e^(β·f(x)) → min⟨P,C⟩ − εH(P) → F(x) = ⟨T(x), ∇H(x)⟩₊ → Coexistence Risk
01

A framework built on well-calibrated habitat models

We rebuilt the maximum-entropy species distribution model from scratch — as its exact equivalent, a penalized Poisson point process — and fit it for seven European mammals from 57,838 GBIF records.

Figure 1 — Framework & data. Occurrence density and realized climatic niches; the Gibbs→transport→topology pipeline; and spatial cross-validation skill (AUC 0.76–0.92, Boyce up to 0.99).

a · Records

57,838 sightings of bear, wolf, lynx, jackal, boar, roe deer and fox.

b · Niches

Realized climatic niches overlap in a shared cool-temperate envelope.

c · Pipeline

Presence-only → MaxEnt/PPP → transport → connectivity → topology → risk.

d · Skill

Block cross-validation, not naive resubstitution — honest performance.

02

Optimal transport reveals a coherent poleward push

Treating present and future habitat as probability measures, we solve the entropic optimal-transport problem between them. The result is a displacement field: an arrow at every location saying where habitat must go.

Figure 2 — Entropic displacement. Under +2.8 °C (SSP2-4.5), every taxon is pushed poleward (mean shift 484 km). The Coexistence Friction field lights up where that push crosses steep human-pressure gradients — the Alps, Carpathians and farmland edges.

a · Gain vs loss

Bivariate map: blue gains in the north, red losses in the south.

b · Displacement field

Streamlines of the optimal-transport map — habitat flowing north.

c · Poleward intensity

Required shift peaks at mid-latitudes and retreating southern margins.

d · Friction

F(x)=⟨displacement, ∇human-pressure⟩₊ — the conflict-pressure field.

03

Movement funnels into corridors — and the range frays

Optimal transport gives the demand for movement; the landscape decides whether it can be met. Circuit theory routes the demand through real resistance; persistent homology measures the fragmentation.

Figure 3 — Connectivity & topology. A spectral graph-Laplacian solve routes forced movement into discrete conflict corridors and pinch-points; cubical persistent homology shows the range's interior gaps (H₁ loops) rising from 2,389 to 2,536.

a · Current

Climate demand solved as electrical current on the landscape graph.

b · Corridors

Current × human pressure = where animals funnel through people.

c · Persistence

Birth–death of habitat patches and loops across all thresholds.

d · Simplification

Persistence landscapes quantify structural range change.

04

A risk map that reality can check

The three geometric signals fuse into one Coexistence Risk Index — and two independent tests, neither using conflict data, support the mechanism.

Figure 4 — Risk & validation. The risk index localizes to the peri-Alpine arc, Carpathians and Fennoscandian fringe. OT predictions rank-match observed range shifts (ρ=0.64); friction predicts realized human-interface occurrence (permutation p=0.0005).
Out-of-sample validation

Test 1 — mechanism. Splitting each species' records into a 1990–2007 and a 2015–2026 epoch, five of seven taxa shifted poleward exactly as the transport map predicted (Spearman ρ = 0.64). The grey wolf is the lone outlier — its recent range change is driven by legal-protection recolonization, a known non-climate confound, not a model failure.

Test 2 — interface. Coexistence Friction significantly predicts which human-dominated cells actually hold recent wildlife records (permutation p = 0.0005 over 4,618 cells) — despite never being trained on those labels.

05

The result holds up

Higher emissions push habitat farther — but the map of where conflict concentrates barely moves. And the from-scratch solver provably hits the maximum-entropy optimum.

Figure S1 — Robustness & verification. Under SSP5-8.5, shifts grow (mean 714 km) yet the friction pattern is nearly identical to SSP2-4.5 (r = 0.98). The optimal-transport shift is grid-invariant (CV 0.5 %), and empirical feature means match the fitted Gibbs expectations to r = 0.99998 — the solver satisfies its defining constraint.
Methods

Four traditions, one pipeline

CONVEX OPT

MaxEnt = Poisson PPP

From-scratch elastic-net solver (FISTA) for the Gibbs / point-process suitability model.

OPTIMAL TRANSPORT

Entropic Sinkhorn

Displacement field and Wasserstein range-shift between present and future habitat measures.

SPECTRAL GRAPH

Circuit theory

Graph-Laplacian solve routes movement demand into corridors and pinch-points.

TOPOLOGY

Persistent homology

Cubical H₀/H₁ persistence quantifies range fragmentation with stability guarantees.

Data GBIF · WorldClim 2.1 · Global Human Modification Domain Europe · 37,070 cells Scenario SSP2-4.5 · 2081–2100 Warming +2.8 °C All data open, no login

The Geometry of Coexistence. A mechanistic, conflict-data-free framework that reframes human–wildlife conflict forecasting as the geometry of moving measures. Built from openly downloadable data with a fully reproducible pipeline. Aspirational venue: a computational-ecology methods journal.

Reproduce: run_pipeline.sh — MaxEnt/PPP · Sinkhorn OT · spectral connectivity · cubical persistence.
